coredata.dc:title: In vitro degradation and biocompatibility of in-situ fabricated Mg-Al-Ga-LDH/MAO hybrid coating on Mg alloy AZ31

coredata.dc:description: A novel antibacterial Mg-Al-Ga layered double hydroxide (LDH) coating was fabricated on micro-arc oxide (MAO) coated magnesium (Mg) alloy AZ31 via low-temperature water bath treatment. Electrochemical polarization and impedance spectroscopy, hydrogen evolution rates, and pH monitoring tests were used to characterize the degradation behaviors. The morphology, compositions, and microstructures of the samples were investigated using scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Antibacterial activities of the coatings against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli were evaluated by plate-counting method. Cell-Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was applied to evaluate the cytotoxicity of coatings. The results demonstrate that the Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating with a nanosheet structure is constructed to blanket the porous MAO coating. Electrochemical and hydrogen evolution tests reveal that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating exhibits superior corrosion resistance. Furthermore, antibacterial and cytotoxicity tests prove that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating possesses an obvious antibacterial and osteogenic effect.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel antibacterial Mg-Al-Ga layered double hydroxide (LDH) coating was fabricated on micro-arc oxide (MAO) coated magnesium (Mg) alloy AZ31 via low-temperature water bath treatment. Electrochemical polarization and impedance spectroscopy, hydrogen evolution rates, and pH monitoring tests were used to characterize the degradation behaviors. The morphology, compositions, and microstructures of the samples were investigated using scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Antibacterial activities of the coatings against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli were evaluated by plate-counting method. Cell-Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was applied to evaluate the cytotoxicity of coatings. The results demonstrate that the Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating with a nanosheet structure is constructed to blanket the porous MAO coating. Electrochemical and hydrogen evolution tests reveal that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating exhibits superior corrosion resistance. Furthermore, antibacterial and cytotoxicity tests prove that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating possesses an obvious antibacterial and osteogenic effect.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium (Mg) alloys, with low density, biodegradability, and adaptive elastic modulus to human bone, have been taken as a promising category of biomedical materials. Compared with traditional inert biomedical metals such as stainless steel, titanium (Ti) alloy, and tantalum metal, Mg alloys as implant biomaterials can automatically degrade after carrying out the service period, which avoids secondary surgery [1–5]. However, the anodic electrode potential of Mg (−2.37 V/SHE) to most engineering metals results in high electrochemical activity and corrosion vulnerability [6,7]. Rapid and excess corrosion is detrimental to the mechanical integrity of Mg-based bone implant material during its in-service life. Moreover, high corrosion rates can cause local alkalinity and hydrogen production to result in inflammation [3,8].

Micro-arc oxidation (MAO), one of the surface modification methods, can provide superior protection to Mg substrates in the initial stage of service life. MAO grows a ceramic oxide film on a metal surface in an electrolyte under high voltage, which has excellent adhesion to its substrate and corrosion resistance [9–11]. Unfortunately, the inherent porous structure of MAO coating is a roadblock to its applications. Aggressive ingredients in corrosive environments are readily approached and attack the underlying metal through the through-pores of MAO coatings, which is deleterious for long-term corrosion resistance [8,10,12]. Therefore, sealing the pores with appropriate post-treatments is a common strategy to improve the protective function of MAO coatings.

MgAl layered double hydroxide (LDH) coating, with a three-dimensional nanosheet structure and ion exchangeability, is a good candidate [13,14]. It not only provides a physical barrier to block the corrosive medium, but also exchanges with anions (e.g., Cl−) in corrosive media, weakening the damage of Cl− to the passivation film, and extending the protection period of the coating [15–17]. Moreover, the positively charged brucite-like layers allow LDHs with various electronic structures and specific surface properties to be synthesized [18]. For example, by incorporating Gd3+ cations in the LDH nanosheets, simultaneous MRI imaging and ischemia-reperfusion therapy should be possible [19].

In addition to the corrosion challenges, Mg alloys, as bone implant materials, suffer bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation, and eventually infection. Mg2+ ions have a certain bactericidal effect, but it is still far from enough. Because the antibacterial effect of Mg alloys is mainly ascribed to the increased pH value, which may be eliminated once its anticorrosion activity improves [20]. Therefore, coatings possessing both anticorrosion and antibacterial effects are needed when used as implantations. At present, the introduction of metallic ions such as Ag+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ ions into the coatings as bactericides is more widespread research. For example, Zou et al. [21] loaded Zn2+ ions into montmorillonite by ion exchange, and then prepared a Zn-loaded montmorillonite coating on the surface of AZ31 Mg alloy by hydrothermal method, suggesting excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. Lei et al. [22] in situ constructed an outer copper-doped zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-8 and inner hydroxyapatite (Cu@ZIF-8/HA) composite coating on AZ31B Mg alloy, which exhibited excellent antibacterial effect due to the release of copper ions and zinc ions from the coating dissolved in bacterial culture solution. However, whether it is Ag+, Cu2+ or Zn2+ ions, there is a large potential difference from the α-Mg matrix. These cations doped into the coating will form galvanic corrosion, which is not conducive to the protection of the Mg substrate [21,23,24].

Gallium (Ga), whose electrode potential is more negative than the above metal ions, has a closer electrode potential to α-Mg. Furthermore, Ga3+ ions have been reported to promote osteogenesis and exhibit an antibacterial effect. As a result of the similarities between Fe3+ and Ga3+, Ga3+ can inhibit the biological activity of iron-binding proteins by bonding with them instead of Fe. Kurtuldu et al. [25] synthesized Ga3+ doping mesoporous bioactive glass nanoparticles by sol-gel method, which exhibited lower viabilities of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria than the control group. Sathish et al. [26] found that ZnO nanoparticles with moderate Ga3+ doping exhibited higher antibacterial activity to E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae and S. aureus. Although some Ga3+-loaded antibacterial products were prepared and exhibited excellent antibacterial effects, few studies mentioned the preparation of Ga3+-containing coatings on Mg alloys. Moreover, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings fabricated in this study possessed both excellent antibacterial and anticorrosion effects, which could better meet the demands of Mg alloy for medical use.

In our previous work, we achieved excellent corrosion-resistant MAO/Mg-Al-LDH composite coatings by the assisted promotion of EDTA prepared at low temperatures and high pH values [27]. Based on and inspired by this work, we try to develop a green and applicable metal cation-doped LDH coating technology to endow LDH with multiple functions. In the chemical periodic table, Ga and Al elements belong to the same group IIIA and they have similar chemical properties. Therefore, we first chose the element Ga as the main object of the study. These attempts and studies may give the LDH coating better biocompatibility and antibacterial properties.

The as-extruded Mg alloy AZ31 was selected as the substrate and supplied by the Shandong Yin Guang Yu Yuan Light Metal Precision Molding Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were from Qingdao Jingke Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Before the experiment, the substrates were cut into a cuboid with a dimension of 2 cm × 2 cm × 0.5 cm. They were ground with SiC sandpaper from 150 to 1500 grits, rinsed separately with distilled (DI) water and absolute ethanol, and then dried with warm air and stored in sealed culture glass containers.

An MAO device consisting of an HNMAO-20A-DPM400 power supply and a PMMA-made electrolytic cell equipped with a stainless-steel plate was applied to prepare the MAO coatings. Also, the bare AZ31 and stainless-steel plate were regarded as the anode and the cathode, respectively. The electrolyte consisted of 6 g/L of phytic acid and 10 g/L of NaOH [28]. The frequency, duty cycle, current density, and oxidation time were fixed at 500 Hz, 20 %, 83.3 mA/cm, and 3 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: NaOH and EDTA-2Na, with a concentration of 1, 0.03 M, respectively, were dissolved in DI water, designated as Solution A. Then, 0.05 M Ga(NO3)3·9H2O was added to Solution A. Finally, MAO-coated AZ31 samples were placed in the above solution mixture at 60 °C for 48 h and the sample was defined as MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH. The schematic representations of the preparation process of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coating are shown in Fig. 1 .

The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of the MAO and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Nova NanoSEM 450, US). The elemental compositions were identified using energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS). The crystallographic structures of the bare AZ31, MAO, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings were detected using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX2500PC, Japan) with a Cu target (λ = 0.154 nm) at 3° min−1 scanning rate from 5° to 80° over a 2θ range. The chemical functional groups and chemical components of MAO, MAO/Mg-Al-LDH coatings, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 380, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). And the chemical compositions of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating were also observed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250Xi, Thermo VG Co, East Sussex, US) with an Al Kα X-ray source.

The potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using an electrochemical device (VersaSTAT 4, USA) were applied to evaluate the corrosion resistance of the bare AZ31, MAO, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings. The samples were tested with a conventional three-electrode device placed into Hank's solution (8.0 g/L NaCl, 1.0 g/L C6H6O6, 0.35 g/L NaHCO3, 0.4 g/L KCl, 0.14 g/L CaCl2, 0.1 g/L MgCl2·6H2O, 0.06 g/L MgSO4·7H2O, 0.06 g/L KH2PO4 and 0.06 g/L Na2HPO4·12H2O) at room temperature. The bare and coated AZ31, a saturated calomel electrode, and a reference electrode were regarded as a working electrode, a platinum electrode, and a counter electrode, respectively. The PDP curves of the samples were obtained at a scan rate of 2 mV/s. The impedance was tested with an interference potential of 10 mV in the range of 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz.

The volumetric hydrogen evolution measurement technique was applied to evaluate the corrosion trends of the bare AZ31, MAO, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings over long periods in Hank's solutions at 37.5 ± 0.1 °C [27,29]. The device consisted of a funnel connected to an acid burette inverted in a beaker. The sample was placed under a funnel. The solution scale in the burette was recorded intermittently. Three parallel samples in each group were tested.

The antibacterial activity of MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings was evaluated using a plate counting method, in which Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were used as representative colonies. The bacterial suspension was diluted to 107 colony forming units (CFU)·mL−1 with liquid medium and co-cultured with MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coated samples at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 22 h, after that the co-cultured suspension pH was detected and the suspension was diluted 1000 times with 0.85 % NaCl solution, and 20 μL of it was inoculated onto agar medium. At last, the petri dish was placed in a 37 °C thermostatic incubator for 20 h and then removed. And the number of bacteria alive was counted and the antibacterial rate was calculated [30].

Cell-Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was applied to evaluate the SDBMSC proliferation at 1 and 3 days after incubation. SDBMSCs were firstly seeded into the 96-well plate with 5 × 103 cells/well and then cultured by culture medium for 24 h. After that, the original medium was replaced by 100 μL/well mixed medium (10 % culture medium and 20 % diluted sample extract), in which the cells were continuously cultured for 1 and 3 days. At last, the absorbance OD value of cells at 450 nm was measured with an enzyme marker after 3 h co-cultured with 10 μL/well CCK-8 solution. Moreover, the living/dead cell staining experiment was used to test the cytotoxicity. The living and dead cells were stained green and red with calcein AM and ethidium homodimer respectively and were observed and photographed with a fluorescence microscope.

All the values from antibacterial and cytotoxicity tests were presented as mean ± standard deviation. t-tests were conducted to analyze the statistical significance of the results and p-value <0.05 was considered as an observed difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface characterization of coated Mg alloy AZ31
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the surface morphology images of the MAO and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings. As seen from Fig. 2a and b, a typical porous morphology with microcracks was observed on the surface of the MAO coating. Also, from Fig. 2c and d, the micropores and microcracks on the surface of the MAO coating disappeared, whereas the surface of the MAO coating was completely covered by rosette-like nanosheets. The elemental compositions, detected using EDS in Table S2, displayed that the presence of elemental P implied the phytic acid participated in the formation of the MAO coating. For the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating, the decrease in the content of elemental P suggested that the MAO coating was covered. Moreover, the presence of the elemental Al and Ga implied that the elemental Al in the AZ31 substrate and elemental Ga in the solution participated in the formation of the LDH coating. However, the Mg/Al atomic ratio was about 9.92:1 much larger than 3:1, for which some of the Mg2+ ions in Mg(OH)2 were replaced by Ga3+ due to the introduction of Ga3+ ions. The results showed that Ga3+ ions were successfully doped into the top layer of LDH.

The cross-sectional views of the (a) MAO and (b) MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings, and the corresponding mapping images of the elemental Mg, O, P, Al, and Ga are demonstrated in Fig. 3 . As can be noted from Fig. 3a, the thickness of the MAO coating is 3.59 ± 0.46 μm, which shows a porous and rough structure. A small amount of epoxy resin is inserted into the micropores of the MAO coating. For the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coating, the thickness of the LDH coating is 1.61 ± 0.17 μm, which is completely embedded in the MAO coating. However, the thickness of the MAO part of the composite coating is only 3.04 ± 0.38 μm. The decrease in coating thickness may be due to the partial dissolution of the MAO coating during the preparation of the LDH coating. However, the Mg2+ and OH− generated by the MAO dissolution will promote the formation of LDH. Despite the appearance of cracks and corrosion pits after MAO dissolution, as can be seen from the cross-sectional scanning image (Fig. 3), MAO only partially dissolve and become thinner, and the final formed MAO/LDH coating is thicker than that of pure MAO coating, which proved that the rate of LDH growth exceeds the rate of MAO coating dissolution.

The XRD patterns of the (a) bare AZ31, (b) MAO, and (c) MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings are illustrated in Fig. 4 . The diffraction peaks of α-Mg and MgO are observed in all samples. For the bare AZ31 (Fig. 4a), the appearance of MgO is owing to oxidation during the storage period; for the MAO coating, the appearance of MgO symbolizes the successful preparation of MAO coatings; for the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating, the decrease of MgO diffraction peak height implies MAO coating is covered. Any diffraction peaks of phosphate are not observed in MAO coating, owing to too low crystallinity. In addition, the diffraction peaks corresponding to LDH at 2θ = 10.95° (003) and 22.09° (006) are observed in Fig. 4c, which shows the successful preparation of LDH. However, its diffraction angle is smaller than that of Mg-Al-LDH of the diffraction peaks position of (003)/(006). The reason may be due to the doping of Ga3+, the radius of Ga3+ is larger than that of Al3+. Thence, Ga3+-doped Mg-Al-LDH crystal has a larger interplanar spacing. According to the Bragg formula [31,32]: 2dsinθ = nλ, the diffraction angle of Ga3+-doped Mg-Al-LDH becomes small. This also further proves that Ga3+ was successfully doped into the top layer of the LDH coating.

The chemical functional groups and chemical components of MAO, MAO/Mg-Al-LDH coatings, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings were analyzed by FT-IR in Fig. S1. The peaks at 3697 and 3450 cm−1 were assigned to Mg-OH and OH stretching vibrations, evidencing the formation of Mg(OH)2 and other metal hydroxides. Besides, the double peaks at 1351 and 1384 cm−1, and the peaks at 1114 cm−1 corresponded to CO3 2− and CO, which were due to the presence of CO3 2− intercalation in LDH. The peaks that appeared at 1640 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetric H2O [33,34]. In addition, the two peaks of 767 cm−1 and 620 cm−1 are metal oxide peaks, which may be small amounts of AlO and MgO bonds. Compared with MAO coatings, there was a significant Mg-OH peak at 3697 cm−1 in MAO/Mg-Al-LDH and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings, which also proved the successful preparation of LDH coatings.

XPS is employed to further analyze the surface compositions of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coating. The MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating survey plots are shown in Fig. 5a. The curves fitting of the high-resolution (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Al 2p, (e) Ga 3/2p, and (f) Mg 1s peaks of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating are performed in Fig. 5. The spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 5a) is decomposed into three peaks: CC (284.6 eV), CN (286 eV) and CO3 2− (289 eV) [35]. The group of the CN corresponds to the EDTA. The spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 5c) is differentiated into two peaks, that is, CN (399 eV) and NO3 − (397.6 eV) [36]. The groups of CO3 2− and NO3 − are observed in Fig. 5b and c, which implies that the intercalation anions in LDH are CO3 2− and NO3 −. And the spectrum of Al 2p (Fig. 5d) consists of Al(OH)3 (74.0 eV) and Mg-Al-LDH (74.55 eV) groups [37,38], which indicates that Al(OH)3 and Mg-Al-LDH are formed in the LDH coating. The peak of Ga 3/2p (Fig. 5e) is distinguished into two peaks, corresponding to Ga2O3 (1117.5 eV) and Mg-Ga-LDH (1118 eV) groups [39–41]. This reveals that Ga3+ are successfully introduced into the composite coating and exist in the form of oxide and LDH in the LDH coating, whose mechanism will be explained in detail in the discussion section. The spectrum of Mg 1s (Fig. 5f) is assigned to Mg(OH)2 (1302.7 eV), MgAl LDH (1304 eV), and MgGa LDH (1304.8 eV) groups. This also proves the formation of Mg-Al-LDH and Mg-Ga-LDH.

Fig. 6 shows the results of EIS of the (I) bare AZ31, (II) MAO, and (III) MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating. Fig. 6b and c perform the Bode and Bode phase angle plots. Figs. 6c-e illustrate the Nyquist plots and the corresponding equivalent circuits (ECs). The greater the low frequency (at 0.01 Hz) impedance modulus (|Z|) is, the better the corrosion resistance is. In the Bode plot of Fig. 6b, at the frequency of 0.01 Hz, the |Z| value of MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating is 3.34 × 105 Ω·cm2, higher than that of the MAO coating (4.20 × 104 Ω·cm2), and far larger than that of the bare AZ31 (6.22 × 102 Ω·cm2). This suggests that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating has the best corrosion resistance. In addition, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating has at least two-time constants in the Bode phase angle plot (Fig. 6b), which implies the multilayer structure. Also, the larger impedance loop at low-frequency signifies better corrosion resistance. As can be seen from Fig. 6e, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating has the largest semicircle diameter, which confirms that the composite coating has the best corrosion resistance.

To better explore the protection provided by the coating to the substrate, ZSimpWin software is employed to analyze and fit the ECs of the EIS in Figs. 6c-e. The results of the fitting data are displayed in Table S1. In the ECs, the R s signifies the Ohmic resistance, that is, solution resistance, and R ct charge transfer resistance. The CPE represents the constant phase elements, which indicates the deviation of the pure resistance C from the theoretical value; n as a dimensionless coefficient is introduced to fix this deviation. If n is equal to 1, the CPE is the same as the pure capacitance C. The C dl and CPE dl represent a double-layer capacitance of the coating, and R 1 and R 2 are the electrical resistance of the corresponding coating. As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the CPE dl and R ct at the high-frequency region represent a loose and porous corrosion product film on the surface of the bare AZ31. A low-frequency capacitor loop composed of the inductance L and resistance R L signifies the pitting and shedding-corrosion products of the bare AZ31. The MAO coating shows a two-layer structure, that is, a loose layer composed of the resistance (R 1) and a constant phase component (CPE 1), and a dense layer resistance (R 2) and a constant phase component (CPE 2). However, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating consists of the outer layer of the LDH coating (R 1 and CPE 1) and the inner layer of the MAO coating (R 2 and CPE 2). It can be found that the inner layer of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating, that is, MAO coating, does not have a two-layer structure, which can be due to the sealing of the LDH coating for the loose layer of the MAO coating. Moreover, the coated AZ31 also possesses a double-layer capacitance (CPE dl) and the corresponding charge transfer resistance (R ct). Z w represents the diffusion resistance of the LDH coating, which implies that the LDH coating has an ion-exchangeable capacity. The larger R ct value represents the better the corrosion resistance. In Table S1, the R ct values are the following order: bare AZ31 (9.81 × 102 Ω·cm2) < MAO coating (3.89 × 104 Ω·cm2) < MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating (2.10 × 105 Ω·cm2). This discloses that MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating has the best corrosion resistance.

The PDP curves and the results of the corrosion current density (i corr) and corrosion potential (E corr) of the (I) bare AZ31, (II) MAO, and (III) MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating are demonstrated in Fig. 7 . In PDP curves, MAO coating possesses lower E
corr than bare AZ31, that is, MAO coating is more prone to corrosion in thermodynamics, which may be related to the large potential difference between MAO and AZ31 substrate and the porous structure of MAO coating [42]. However, the i
corr , as a kinetic parameter, can be used to characterize the corrosion rate directly. The smaller i corr represents the better the corrosion resistance. As can be observed from Fig. 7b, the i corr values of all samples are in the following order: Mg alloy AZ31 (1.78× 10−5 A·cm−2) > MAO coating (6.98 × 10−7 A·cm−2) > MAO-LDH coating (4.31 × 10−8 A·cm−2). This discloses that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating, with the smallest i corr, has the best corrosion resistance. MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating also has a wider passivation interval and higher blunt potential than that of the substrate and MAO coating. This implies an increase in the pitting resistance of the coating, which can provide better protection to the substrate. The reason may be that LDH coating has an ion-exchange capacity. When Cl− in the corrosive medium diffuses to the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating surface, it can exchange with the anions (e.g., CO3 2−) in the LDH coating, which is inserted into the LDH coating [27]. This avoids that Cl− adsorbed on the coating surface, forming soluble chloride and damaging the coating. As in our previous research [27], the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating also has a certain self-healing ability which is observed in the anode region.

To observe the corrosion tendency of all samples in corrosive media, the volumetric hydrogen evolution measurement technique is applied in the soaked Hank's solution for 156 h. The hydrogen evolution rate (HER) curves are illustrated in Fig. 8 . As can be observed from Fig. 8, at the initial stage of immersion, that is, before 1 h, for the bare AZ31, the HER raised rapidly. This is because α-Mg of the substrate is first corroded. Within 1–20 h, the HER gradually decreases and tends towards balance because corrosion product film is formed on the substrate surface to inhibit corrosion. Within about 20–60 h, the HER raises again, which implies that the corrosion product of the surface of the bare AZ31 is dissolved and the substrate is again exposed to Hank's solution. After 60 h, the HER decreases and tends towards balance again, which shows a new corrosion product film is formed at the exposed portion of the substrate and gradually thickened to provide better protection to the substrate. The corrosion rate of bare AZ31 The MAO and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings have a similar corrosion tendency as the substrate. However, the average corrosion rate of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating is lower than that of the MAO coating and far lower than that of the substrate. The results of the HER curves indicate the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating can provide the best protection to the substrate.

The macro and microtopography images of the (a-c) bare AZ31, (d-f) MAO, and (g-i) MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating in Hank's solutions for 154 h are demonstrated in Fig. 9 . Too much rough surface is observed on the bare AZ31 (Fig. 9a). And the dry riverbed morphology is observed on the substrate surface (Fig. 9b and c). What's more, white corrosion products are deposited on the bare AZ31 surface. For the MAO coating, the sample edges are severely damaged (Fig. 9d). From Fig. 9e, many cracks are observed in the MAO coating. However, the micropore size of the MAO coating decreases due to the deposition and sealing of corrosion products. Furthermore, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating maintains a smooth and flat surface (Fig. 9g). As can be observed from Fig. 9h and i, the surface yields many microcracks, however, they are smaller than that of the MAO coating. Of course, many corrosion products are also deposited in the nanosheet structure, which can play a sealing role for the LDH coating to provide protection again.

Table S3 shows the elemental contents of the bare AZ31, MAO coating, and MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating after immersing in Hank's solution for 156 h. As can be seen from Table S3, elemental Ca and P are deposited on the surface of all samples, which implies the corrosion products in Fig. 9 are CaP products. On the other hand, the bare AZ31 and MAO coating have a low elemental content of Cl, whereas the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating has a high elemental content of Cl. This confirms that LDH has an ion-exchange ability with Cl−. And it also further reveals that the reason for the higher pitting potential of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating (Fig. 7) is that Cl− ions are exchanged into the LDH intercalation, which reduces the formation of soluble chloride resulting in damaging the coating.

The antibacterial activity of MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coatings was evaluated using a plate counting method in Fig. 10 and Fig. S2. It was confirmed in Fig. 10b that the anti-E. coli rate of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating reached approximately 99.8 %, 97.2 %, and 76.6 % for 1 h, 3 h, and 5 h, respectively. Simultaneously, the anti-E. coli rate of MAO/Mg-Al-LDH reached about 95.4 %, 82.4 %, and 93.7 % at 1, 3, and 5 h, respectively. It could be seen that the antibacterial effect of Ga-containing coatings was gradually lower than Ga-free coatings with co-culturing. From Fig. S2a-c, similar results can be drawn for the antibacterial effect of S. aureus. MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH exhibits a lower pH value and higher antibacterial activity, further confirming the antibacterial activity of Ga3+ rather than just the effect of alkaline environments. The fluctuation of the antibacterial rate here ought to ascribe to the fluctuation of Ga3+ content in the coating, but the scenario that E. coli is more sensitive to the antibacterial effect of Ga3+ is not excluded.

The viability of SDBMSC cells with different samples was detected using CCK-8 assay (Fig. S3a). In comparison with the control group, all the other groups exhibited evident facilitation to the SDBMSC cell proliferation after 24 and 72 h co-culturing. Among the experienced group, the AZ31 group showed the strongest promotion effect (114 % at 24 h and 124 % at 72 h), which may be due to the fastest Mg2+ ions release rate. As the osteogenic effect of the Mg2+ ions, the faster the Mg2+ ions release rate may lead to a stronger promotion effect. The MAO/Mg-Al-LDH group possessed almost the same promotion effect (109 % at 24 h and 111 % at 72 h) as the MAO group (109 % at 24 h and 108 % at 72 h), illustrating that the LDH layer had little effect on the cell viability. Significantly, the cells of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH group survived more (116 % at 24 h and 72 h) than the MAO/Mg-Al-LDH group, which might indicate that Ga3+ ions promote the proliferation of SDBMSC cells. In addition, as depicted in fluorescence microscopy images (Fig. S3b), the cells of all the groups displayed uniform distribution and healthy shape.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of LDH doped with different metal cations
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, any divalent cation could substitute for Mg2+ in the brucite-like layer. Equally as well, any trivalent cation may substitute for Al3+ in the brucite layer. In LDH a broad range of compositions are possible of the type

During the soaking process, Mg2+ is more easily adsorbed onto the surface of MAO to form hydroxides compared to Ga3+ and Al3+. There are two main destinations for Ga3+ in the formation of MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH: a small amount of Ga3+ is adsorbed onto the surface of MAO to form Ga(OH)3 (Eq. (1)), which is the source of Ga2O3; In the presence of CO3 2−, some of the Mg2+ that forms Mg(OH)2 is replaced by Ga3+ to form Ga(OH)3 and participates in the formation of LDH (Eqs. (2)–(5)).

To verify the wide applicability of different cation-doped LDH preparation techniques, we tried to dope divalent ions [43], even monovalent ions [18], into the coatings by using the same reaction conditions as for the preparation of Mg-Al-Ga-LDH. Here, the concentrations of Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Ag+ were controlled at 0.05 M and reacted under the promotion of EDTA at 60 °C for 48 h. The corresponding SEM and EDS images are shown in Fig. 11 . From the morphology images (Fig. 11a-d), it can be found that almost all the surfaces generated flake structures. As can be seen from the elemental content statistics in Fig. 11f, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Ag+ ions were successfully doped into the LDH coating, which verifies the wide practicality of our developed EDTA-assisted growth of multiple cation-doped LDH technology. In addition, it can be found that the content of divalent ions is relatively low, whereas, the content of trivalent and monovalent ions is high. The reason for the lower content of divalent ions may be due to the greater competition between divalent ions and Al3+ during the LDH growth process. When Al3+ ions replace the Mg2+ ions in the Mg(OH)2 lattice, Al(OH)3 is preferentially generated, its concentration is relatively low, and the reaction proceeds more easily. However, when divalent ions generate hydroxides, the concentration is relatively high due to the large amount of Mg(OH)2, which inhibits the reaction. In other words, the preparation of Mg-Al-LDH doped with Cu2+ and Zn2+ on the surface of the aluminum substrate may be easier to achieve. Surprisingly, monovalent Ag+ was also able to be doped into the LDH coating, which is an interesting phenomenon. This still needs a lot of work to understand the corresponding growth mechanism. To sum up, a green technique suitable for doping multiple cations into LDH coatings was developed by EDTA chelator-assisted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation mechanism of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating
ce:section.ce:para: Predominating the degradation mechanism of the coating and the substrate is essential for the protection of the substrate and the control of corrosion. Based on the analysis of the PDP and HER curves, corrosion morphology images, and elemental content change as well, the degradation mechanism of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating is proposed in Fig. 12 .

In the first stage of corrosion (Fig. 12a-d), LDH has an ion-exchange ability as mentioned earlier, which can effectively reduce the attack from Cl− ions to the coating in the early stage of corrosion. When the samples were immersed in Hank's solution, on the one hand, the Cl− in the solution diffused towards the solution/coating interface and was absorbed and detained by the LDH intercalation (Fig. 12a, b and Eq. 6). This avoided that soluble chlorides were formed in the coating, resulting in any damage to the coating.
(6)
LDH −
CO 3
2 −
+ 2
Cl −
→ LDH − 2
Cl −
+
CO 3
2 −

On the other hand, the released anion CO3 2− in the LDH intercalation diffused to the coating/solution interface and concentrated on the coating surface to form a diffusion boundary layer (Fig. 12b). The concentration of CO3 2− was higher at the coating/solution interface, which was cationic corrosion inhibitors (e.g., Mg2+). Through competitive adsorption, this weakens the degree that Cl− in the solution diffuses into the solution/coating interface, resulting in reduced Cl− damage to the coating.

As the immersion time increases, the damage of the coating (e.g., pitting) initiates at the weak point of the coating (such as near the AlMn phase) [36,44]. At this time, the CO3 2− in the diffusion boundary layer will react with the dissolved Mg2+ to form insoluble precipitate MgCO3 (Fig. 12c and Eq. (7)), and MgCO3 will be easily converted to lower solubility Mg(OH)2 under alkaline conditions (Fig. 12d and Eq. (8)) [45].
(7)
Mg
2 +
+
CO 3
2 −
→
MgCO 3
↓
(8)
MgCO 3
+ 2
OH −

OH
2
↓ +
CO 3
2 −

The corrosion products Mg(OH)2 cover the pitting corrosion, which forms secondary protection. As the result of the anode region of the PDP curve (Fig. 7a), after reaching the pitting potential, the phenomenon of “breakdown-re-passivation-re-breakdown” is observed, that is, the self-healing of the coating.

In the second stage of corrosion, the H2O molecules in the solution gradually penetrate the LDH/MAO interface and MAO/substrate interface through the defects or through-pores in the coating (usually near the AlMn phase), forming galvanic corrosion (Fig. 12e). The AlMn phase with higher electrode potential serves as the cathode, and the α-Mg with lower electrode potential serves as the anode. α-Mg undergoes a dissolution reaction, producing a large amount of Mg(OH)2 and H2 [46,47]. Due to the volume expansion of Mg(OH)2 and the outward and upward force exerted by H2, the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating is subjected to huge stress, which in turn leads to the coating crack (Fig. 12f). As shown in Fig. 10h, many cracks are observed on the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating surface. At the same time, Ca2+ and PO4 3− in the solution will also react and then be deposited on the coating surface to play a certain sealing and protecting function, as shown in Fig. 9i and Fig. 12f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial mechanism and biocompatibility of the coating
ce:section.ce:para: Within 5 h, the antibacterial rates of MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating to E. coli and S. aureus maintain at about 76.6 %. In previous Ga3+-antibacterial research, Qiao et al. [48] synthesized SrTiO3 nanotubes on the surface of titanium and formed a PDA-Ga layer over the SrTiO3 nanotubes. When tested against E. coli and S. aureus, the coating eliminated almost all bacteria within 24 h, maintained their antibacterial properties for up to 7 days of culture without reduction, and retained approximately 72 % of antibacterial activity in 14 days of culture. Shokri et al. [49] synthesized Zn2+-Ga3+ loaded HAp nanopowders with a simple sol-gel method, which exhibited the antibacterial activity of >68 % and 84 % against E. coli and S. aureus, respectively, at the concentration of 500 μg·mL− 1. Fig. 13a, b, and c respectively summarize the antibacterial properties, cytotoxicity, and corrosion resistance of Mg alloys doped with metal ions such as Ag+, Cu2+, and Zn2+. By comparison, it can be seen that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating prepared in this study has excellent antibacterial activity, biocompatibility and corrosion resistance.

According to the above experimental results, both coatings showed an obvious antibacterial effect, and the antibacterial effect of Ga-containing coatings was gradually lower than Ga-free coatings with co-culturing. There may be two antibacterial mechanisms, one is that Ga3+ can interrupt bacterial growth via improved reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in cells due to the generation of Ga3+ ions penetration and intracellular diffusion, which hinders bacterial division and proliferation [49]. And the other is that Ga3+ participates in and interferes with the iron metabolism process of bacteria. Ga3+ has a certain similarity with Fe3+, which is involved in some metabolic pathways of bacteria. Attributed to the similarities with Fe3+, Ga3+ can be adsorbed by bacterial Fe-binding proteins, whereas it cannot be reduced during bacterial metabolism, thereby achieving excellent antibacterial effects [60]. The Ga3+ in this study may be recognized by bacteria as Fe3+ and activate their iron metabolism, blocking the process of iron reduction during iron metabolism, ultimately leading to a decrease or even inactivation of bacterial activity.

It is well known that the activity of E. coli and S. aureus is greatly affected by the pH value of the environment. In this experiment, due to the corrosion of the coating, the pH value of the leaching solution continued to increase, and the antibacterial activity at the initial stage of soaking mainly came from the release of Ga3+, and with the increase of pH value, the effect of pH value gradually strengthened. Whereas the Ga-free coating exhibited poor corrosion resistance and a faster pH rise, which also explained its rapidly increasing antimicrobial rate.
In addition, the introduction of Ga3+ led to a faster SDBMSCs proliferation rate, which was related to the osteogenic effect of Ga3+.

An EDTA-assisted and green technology for LDH doping of Ga3+ was developed on MAO-coated Mg alloy surfaces.
1)
The MAO surface was completely covered and sealed by a rosette-like nanosheet structure. Metal cations were successfully introduced into the Mg-Al-LDH coating. Taking Ga3+ doped Mg-Al-LDH as an example, the ionic radius of Ga3+ was larger than that of Al3+ leading to a shift of the diffraction angle of Mg-Al-LDH to the left.
2)
Electrochemical and hydrogen evolution tests revealed that the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating exhibited superior corrosion resistance. The anion exchangeability of LDH further improved the corrosion resistance of the composite coating, and the sheet structure of LDH was more conducive to the deposition of CaP products.
3)
The MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coating exhibited obvious antibacterial properties against E. coli and S. aureus, especially in the early stage of bacterial culture. Metallic ion-doped LDH growth technology was widely applicable and scalable, and the degradation mechanism of the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH composite coating was proposed.
4)
The SDBMSC cells co-cultured on the MAO/Mg-Al-Ga-LDH coating displayed uniform distribution and healthy shape, and the cell viability reached up to 116 %, indicating a promising coating in promoting cell growth.
The findings may provide a novel insight into the development of metal cation-doped LDH coatings.
